title: Post-Viral & Post-Infectious Illnesses: Current Treatment Options
description: A curated, reference-backed overview of illnesses that persist or emerge after an acute infection — covering the post-acute infection syndromes catalogued by Choutka et al. (Nature Medicine, 2022): Long COVID, post-viral ME/CFS, post-SARS syndrome, post-dengue, post-polio, chronic chikungunya arthritis, post-Ebola, post–West Nile, Ross River virus disease, Q fever fatigue syndrome, post-giardiasis fatigue/IBS, post-influenza (incl. H1N1-associated narcolepsy), post-herpetic neuralgia, post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome, post-enteroviral syndrome, post-infectious POTS, post-Zika Guillain-Barré, and PANS/PANDAS — with the treatment options supported by current evidence. Most post-viral illnesses have no cure; management is largely supportive, rehabilitative, and symptom-directed. Every reference links to a real published article, guideline, or authoritative resource.
disclaimer: For research and educational use only. This is not medical advice and does not replace individualized care from a qualified clinician. Treatment evidence for most post-viral syndromes is limited; inclusion of a therapy does not imply endorsement or proven efficacy.

conditions.name: Long COVID (Post-COVID-19 Condition / PASC)
conditions.typical_onset: Symptoms present or new ≥4 weeks after acute COVID-19, persisting ≥12 weeks for 'post-COVID-19 syndrome'.
conditions.overview: A multi-system condition with fatigue, post-exertional malaise, dyspnea, cognitive dysfunction ('brain fog'), autonomic symptoms, and more. The most-studied post-viral illness; management is multidisciplinary and symptom-directed, with one drug (metformin, given early in acute infection) shown to reduce the incidence of Long COVID.
conditions.epidemiology: Affects a substantial minority of people after SARS-CoV-2 infection; the single largest post-viral illness cohort in history.
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, after excluding alternative diagnoses; NICE defines acute (≤4 wk), ongoing symptomatic (4–12 wk), and post-COVID-19 syndrome (>12 wk).

treatments.name: Multidisciplinary rehabilitation & self-management (NICE NG188)
treatments.summary: NICE/SIGN/RCGP recommend holistic assessment and multidisciplinary, symptom-guided rehabilitation (physical, psychological, psychiatric) with self-management support. Care must respect post-exertional symptom exacerbation.

treatments.name: Metformin during acute infection (secondary prevention)
treatments.summary: In the COVID-OUT phase 3 RCT, a 14-day course of metformin started early in acute COVID-19 reduced cumulative Long COVID incidence at 10 months (6.3% vs 10.4%); benefit was larger when started <4 days from symptom onset. Ivermectin and fluvoxamine did not prevent Long COVID.

treatments.name: Energy management / pacing for the ME/CFS-like phenotype
treatments.summary: For patients with post-exertional malaise, pacing (activity within an energy envelope) is used, mirroring ME/CFS guidance. Graded exercise therapy is not recommended where PEM is present.

references.title: COVID-19 rapid guideline: managing the long-term effects of COVID-19 (NG188)
references.source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (UK)

references.title: Outpatient treatment of COVID-19 and incidence of post-COVID-19 condition over 10 months (COVID-OUT): a multicentre, randomised, quadruple-blind, parallel-group, phase 3 trial
Myalgic encephalomyelitis (or encephalopathy)/chronic fatigue syndrome: diagnosis and management (NG206)
references.source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (UK)

conditions.name: Post-Viral Fatigue Syndrome & ME/CFS
conditions.typical_onset: Fatigue and post-exertional malaise persisting ≥3–6 months after an acute infection.
conditions.overview: A prospectively documented syndrome in which a stereotyped fatigue state follows diverse infections. The Dubbo cohort showed ~11% of people develop a prolonged fatigue syndrome 6 months after EBV, Ross River virus, or Q fever, with risk predicted by acute illness severity rather than pathogen. Management centers on energy management (pacing); graded exercise therapy is no longer recommended.
conditions.epidemiology: A consistent minority (~10–12%) across pathogens develop post-infective fatigue at 6 months (Dubbo study).
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical criteria (e.g., IOM/NICE); requires post-exertional malaise and exclusion of alternative causes.

treatments.name: Energy management (pacing)
treatments.summary: NICE NG206 recommends individualized energy management within an 'energy envelope' and explicitly recommends against offering graded exercise therapy as a treatment.

treatments.name: Symptom-directed care (sleep, pain, orthostatic intolerance)
treatments.summary: There is no curative therapy; guidelines support treating individual symptoms (sleep disturbance, pain, orthostatic intolerance) and managing comorbidities.

treatments.name: No proven pathogen-eradicating or curative drug
treatments.summary: Prospective data (Dubbo) found post-infective fatigue is not explained by ongoing detectable infection or a single cytokine signature, and no antimicrobial or immunomodulatory cure is established.

references.title: Post-infective and chronic fatigue syndromes precipitated by viral and non-viral pathogens: prospective cohort study
Myalgic encephalomyelitis (or encephalopathy)/chronic fatigue syndrome: diagnosis and management (NG206)
references.source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (UK)

conditions.name: Post-Dengue Fatigue Syndrome
conditions.typical_onset: Fatigue and related symptoms persisting weeks to months after acute dengue.
conditions.overview: A substantial fraction of dengue patients report prolonged fatigue, musculoskeletal pain, and cognitive/mood symptoms during convalescence. Prospective cohorts (e.g., the Colombo Dengue Study) document persistent symptoms in a meaningful minority; management is supportive, as no dengue-specific therapy for the post-viral phase is established.
conditions.epidemiology: Prospective cohorts report fatigue in roughly a quarter to a third of patients at 2 months, with a subset persisting far longer; female sex and older age increase risk.
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, during dengue convalescence, after excluding other causes.

treatments.name: Supportive & symptom-directed care
treatments.summary: No proven disease-specific therapy exists for post-dengue fatigue; care is supportive (graded return to activity, analgesia for arthralgia, sleep and mood support), guided by prospective characterization of the syndrome.

references.title: Dengue and post-infection fatigue: findings from a prospective cohort—the Colombo Dengue Study
references.authors: Sigera PC, Rajapakse S, et al.
references.source: Transactions of the Royal Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene

references.title: Post-infectious fatigue syndrome in dengue infection
references.authors: Seet RCS, Quek AML, Lim ECH

conditions.name: Post-Polio Syndrome
conditions.typical_onset: New weakness, fatigue, and pain emerging decades (typically 15–40 years) after acute paralytic poliomyelitis.
conditions.overview: A late neuromuscular condition in polio survivors marked by new or increasing muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain. Despite multiple trials (IVIG, pyridostigmine, lamotrigine, modafinil, amantadine, prednisone), a Cochrane review found insufficient evidence to confirm any pharmacologic treatment; management is supportive and rehabilitative.
conditions.epidemiology: Affects a large share of paralytic polio survivors; still clinically important given historical polio burden.
mechanisms: Distal degeneration of enlarged motor units / ongoing denervation
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, in a polio survivor with new progressive symptoms, after excluding other causes.

treatments.name: Individualized rehabilitation & energy conservation
treatments.summary: Non-fatiguing exercise, energy conservation, assistive devices, and multidisciplinary rehabilitation are mainstays; the Cochrane review notes non-pharmacologic approaches are commonly used though high-quality trial evidence is limited.

treatments.name: Pharmacologic agents (IVIG, pyridostigmine, lamotrigine, modafinil, amantadine, prednisone)
treatments.summary: The Cochrane review of 10 pharmacologic studies found pyridostigmine not beneficial, IVIG without benefit on activity limitations, and lamotrigine possibly helpful but needing confirmation — overall insufficient evidence to recommend any drug. A separate meta-analysis likewise found IVIG lacked convincing benefit.

references.title: Treatment for postpolio syndrome
Intravenous immunoglobulin for postpolio syndrome: a systematic review and meta-analysis

conditions.name: Chronic Chikungunya Arthritis
conditions.typical_onset: Persistent inflammatory joint pain lasting >3 months after acute chikungunya.
conditions.overview: A chronic, often symmetric inflammatory arthritis that can persist months to years after chikungunya fever. Unlike most post-viral syndromes, there is randomized evidence supporting disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) therapy: combination DMARDs outperform hydroxychloroquine monotherapy, and methotrexate is widely used.
conditions.epidemiology: A large proportion of acute chikungunya patients develop persistent arthralgia; a subset progress to chronic inflammatory arthritis.
mechanisms: Persistent viral antigen in joint tissue
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, in a patient with prior chikungunya and persistent inflammatory joint disease.

treatments.name: Combination DMARD therapy (MTX + sulfasalazine + hydroxychloroquine)
treatments.summary: A 24-week randomized open-label trial found triple DMARD therapy superior to hydroxychloroquine monotherapy for chronic persistent chikungunya arthritis, with better disease-activity and function scores.

treatments.name: Methotrexate
treatments.summary: A systematic review supports methotrexate for chronic chikungunya arthritis, while highlighting the need for larger placebo-controlled monotherapy trials.

references.title: Treatment of Chronic Chikungunya Arthritis With Methotrexate: A Systematic Review
Efficacy of combination DMARD therapy vs. hydroxychloroquine monotherapy in chronic persistent chikungunya arthritis: a 24-week randomized controlled open label study

conditions.name: Post-Ebola Virus Syndrome
conditions.typical_onset: Persistent multi-system symptoms after recovery from acute Ebola virus disease.
conditions.overview: Ebola survivors experience a broad syndrome including arthralgia, headache, fatigue, mental-health effects, and — importantly — ocular disease (uveitis) that can threaten sight, with viral persistence in immune-privileged sites (eye, semen). WHO interim guidance provides a structured follow-up and management framework; uveitis requires prompt ophthalmologic treatment.
conditions.epidemiology: In PREVAIL III, uveitis was common among survivors and associated with more severe acute disease (longer time in the Ebola treatment unit).
mechanisms: Viral persistence in immune-privileged organs (eye, CNS, semen)
conditions.diagnosis: Structured survivor follow-up including eye examination; per WHO interim guidance.

treatments.name: Structured survivor follow-up & symptom management (WHO interim guidance)
treatments.summary: WHO interim guidance recommends planned follow-up addressing common sequelae (ocular, musculoskeletal, neurologic, psychosocial), monitoring for persistent infection, and infection-prevention counseling.

treatments.name: Ophthalmologic treatment of Ebola-associated uveitis
treatments.summary: Uveitis is a leading, potentially blinding complication; the PREVAIL III cohort characterized its burden and risk factors, supporting prompt specialist evaluation and standard anti-inflammatory uveitis therapy.

references.title: Clinical care for survivors of Ebola virus disease: interim guidance
Factors associated with uveitis among Ebola survivors in the PREVAIL III study
An update on ocular complications of Ebola virus disease
references.authors: Shantha JG, Yeh S, Nguyen QD

conditions.name: Post-Infectious POTS (Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome)
conditions.typical_onset: Orthostatic intolerance and tachycardia developing after a viral illness; infection is the most common identified trigger.
conditions.overview: POTS is a disorder of orthostatic intolerance with excessive standing tachycardia; post-infectious onset is the most common mode, and POTS is now frequently reported after COVID-19. Management combines non-pharmacologic measures (fluids, salt, compression, exercise reconditioning) with drugs (beta-blockers, ivabradine, midodrine, fludrocortisone) per expert consensus.
conditions.epidemiology: Reported in a notable fraction of COVID-19 survivors; historically often post-viral.
conditions.diagnosis: Sustained HR rise ≥30 bpm (≥40 in adolescents) within 10 min of standing without orthostatic hypotension, with chronic symptoms.

treatments.name: Non-pharmacologic measures & guideline-based care (HRS consensus)
treatments.summary: The 2015 Heart Rhythm Society expert consensus supports volume expansion (fluids/salt), compression garments, exercise reconditioning, and pharmacotherapy (e.g., propranolol, midodrine, fludrocortisone) individualized to the patient.

treatments.name: Ivabradine
treatments.summary: A randomized trial found ivabradine lowered heart rate and improved quality of life in hyperadrenergic POTS without lowering blood pressure, offering an option when beta-blockers are poorly tolerated.

references.title: 2015 Heart Rhythm Society Expert Consensus Statement on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Postural Tachycardia Syndrome, Inappropriate Sinus Tachycardia, and Vasovagal Syncope
Randomized Trial of Ivabradine in Patients With Hyperadrenergic Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome
references.source: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

conditions.name: PANS / PANDAS (Post-Infectious Neuropsychiatric Syndromes)
aka: pediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorders associated with streptococcal infections
conditions.typical_onset: Abrupt, dramatic onset of OCD and/or food restriction with neuropsychiatric symptoms in children, often after infection.
conditions.overview: A post-infectious neuropsychiatric syndrome in children with sudden-onset OCD, tics, anxiety, and behavioral regression. Consensus guidelines (JCAP 2017) organize treatment into three arms: psychiatric/behavioral therapy, treatment/prevention of infections, and immunomodulatory therapy for moderate-to-severe cases.
conditions.epidemiology: Pediatric; incidence uncertain and debated, but a recognized clinical entity with consensus management.
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical consensus criteria; PANDAS requires temporal association with Group A streptococcal infection.

treatments.name: Psychiatric & behavioral therapy (CBT/ERP + psychotropics)
treatments.summary: JCAP 2017 consensus recommends cognitive behavioral therapy / exposure-response prevention and cautious psychotropic use as foundational symptomatic treatment.

treatments.name: Treatment & prevention of infections (antibiotics)
treatments.summary: The PANS/PANDAS Consortium (JCAP 2017, Part III) provides consensus guidance on identifying, treating, and in selected cases preventing streptococcal and other infections.

treatments.name: Immunomodulatory therapy (NSAIDs, corticosteroids, IVIG, plasmapheresis)
treatments.summary: For moderate-to-severe or refractory cases, JCAP 2017 (Part II) offers consensus recommendations on immunomodulatory therapies targeting neuroinflammation and post-infectious autoimmunity.

references.title: Clinical Management of Pediatric Acute-Onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome: Part II—Use of Immunomodulatory Therapies
references.source: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology

references.title: Clinical Management of Pediatric Acute-Onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome: Part III—Treatment and Prevention of Infections
references.source: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology (PMC)

conditions.name: Post-Zika Guillain-Barré Syndrome
conditions.typical_onset: Acute ascending weakness days to weeks after Zika virus infection.
conditions.overview: Zika virus is an established trigger of Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), an acute immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy. The causal association was demonstrated in a case-control study during the French Polynesia outbreak. Acute treatment follows standard GBS management (IVIG or plasma exchange plus supportive/respiratory care).
conditions.epidemiology: Zika outbreaks were accompanied by surges in GBS; WHO concluded Zika is a GBS trigger.
mechanisms: Post-infectious autoimmune attack on peripheral nerves (molecular mimicry)
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical plus supportive CSF/electrophysiology; recent Zika exposure/serology supports attribution.

treatments.name: Standard GBS immunotherapy (IVIG or plasma exchange) + supportive care
treatments.summary: Zika-associated GBS is treated as classic GBS — IVIG or plasma exchange with close respiratory and autonomic monitoring. The Zika–GBS causal link is established by case-control and multi-country case-series evidence.

references.title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome outbreak associated with Zika virus infection in French Polynesia: a case-control study
Zika Virus and the Guillain–Barré Syndrome — Case Series from Seven Countries

conditions.name: Post-SARS Syndrome (SARS-CoV-1)
conditions.typical_onset: Persistent fatigue, pain, and non-restorative sleep months to years after the 2003 SARS coronavirus infection.
conditions.overview: Survivors of the 2003 SARS-CoV-1 outbreak described a chronic syndrome of fatigue, diffuse myalgia, weakness, depression, and non-restorative sleep resembling fibromyalgia and ME/CFS — an early coronavirus precedent for Long COVID. No disease-specific therapy exists; management is supportive.
conditions.epidemiology: Documented among SARS survivors (notably healthcare workers) unable to return to work 1–3 years after infection.
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, in a SARS survivor with persistent fatigue/pain after excluding alternatives.

treatments.name: Supportive & symptom-directed care
treatments.summary: No disease-specific therapy; management mirrors ME/CFS and fibromyalgia (sleep, pain, and mood support with activity pacing). The syndrome was characterized in a controlled sleep study of post-SARS patients.

references.title: Chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, depression and disordered sleep in chronic post-SARS syndrome; a case-controlled study

conditions.name: Post–West Nile Virus Neurological Sequelae
conditions.typical_onset: Persistent fatigue, cognitive, and motor symptoms for months to years after West Nile neuroinvasive disease.
conditions.overview: After West Nile neuroinvasive disease (meningitis, encephalitis, or acute flaccid paralysis), many patients have prolonged fatigue, cognitive difficulty, weakness, and mood symptoms. There is no specific antiviral; management is supportive care and rehabilitation.
conditions.epidemiology: Over half of neuroinvasive-disease patients report ongoing symptoms >1 year; only ~37% fully recover within 12 months.
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical follow-up after confirmed West Nile neuroinvasive disease.

treatments.name: Supportive care & rehabilitation
treatments.summary: No specific antiviral therapy exists; multidisciplinary rehabilitation and symptom management are the mainstays, informed by prospective longitudinal outcome data documenting the sequelae.

references.title: West Nile virus neuroinvasive disease: neurological manifestations and prospective longitudinal outcomes

conditions.name: Ross River Virus Disease (Epidemic Polyarthritis)
conditions.typical_onset: Polyarthralgia/arthritis with fatigue and rash; most resolve over 3–6 months but arthralgia can persist for years.
conditions.overview: Australia's most common mosquito-borne infection causes an acute polyarthritis with fever, rash, myalgia, and fatigue. Most cases resolve within months, but a subset have prolonged arthralgia and fatigue. Treatment is symptomatic (NSAIDs); no antiviral is available.
conditions.epidemiology: Symptoms typically resolve over 3–6 months; chronic arthralgia and fatigue persist in a minority.
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical plus RRV serology in an endemic setting.

treatments.name: NSAIDs & symptomatic care
treatments.summary: Management is symptomatic; NSAIDs give partial relief and most patients recover over 3–6 months per natural-history data. There is no proven disease-modifying or antiviral therapy.

treatments.name: Pentosan polysulfate sodium
treatments.summary: A phase 2a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study evaluated pentosan polysulfate sodium for Ross River virus–induced arthralgia; remains investigational.

references.title: Natural history of Ross River virus-induced epidemic polyarthritis
Pentosan polysulfate sodium for Ross River virus-induced arthralgia: a phase 2a, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study

conditions.name: Q Fever Fatigue Syndrome (QFS)
conditions.typical_onset: Severe, persistent fatigue lasting ≥6 months after acute Q fever.
conditions.overview: A prolonged fatigue state following acute Q fever. In the randomized Qure trial, long-term doxycycline was no better than placebo, while cognitive behavioural therapy reduced fatigue short-term (benefit not sustained at 1 year) — arguing against an ongoing-infection model and for symptom-directed management.
conditions.epidemiology: A recognized sequela after acute Q fever outbreaks (e.g., the Netherlands 2007–2010).
mechanisms: Post-infectious immune dysregulation (persistent infection not supported by trial data)
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, ≥6 months of fatigue after documented acute Q fever, excluding chronic Q fever and other causes.

treatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)
treatments.summary: In the Qure RCT, CBT significantly reduced fatigue severity versus placebo at end of treatment, but the benefit was not sustained at 1-year follow-up.

treatments.name: Long-term doxycycline
treatments.summary: The Qure RCT found 24 weeks of doxycycline no better than placebo for fatigue severity, arguing against an ongoing-infection basis for QFS.

references.title: Effectiveness of Long-term Doxycycline Treatment and Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy on Fatigue Severity in Patients with Q Fever Fatigue Syndrome (Qure Study): A Randomized Controlled Trial

conditions.name: Post-Giardiasis Fatigue & Post-Infectious IBS
conditions.typical_onset: Chronic fatigue and irritable bowel symptoms persisting years after acute giardiasis.
conditions.overview: Following a large waterborne Giardia outbreak in Bergen, Norway (2004), controlled cohorts documented markedly elevated irritable bowel syndrome and chronic fatigue years later. Management is symptom-directed (IBS-focused care, fatigue management); eradication of Giardia does not reliably resolve the post-infectious syndrome.
conditions.epidemiology: In the Bergen cohort, 6 years after infection IBS prevalence was ~39% and chronic fatigue ~31% in exposed individuals — significantly higher than controls; both remained elevated at 10 years.
mechanisms: Post-infectious gut dysbiosis / mucosal immune activation
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, in a patient with documented prior giardiasis and persistent IBS/fatigue.

treatments.name: Symptom-directed care (IBS management, fatigue support)
treatments.summary: No disease-specific therapy; management targets IBS symptoms and fatigue, informed by controlled prospective cohort data quantifying the long-term risk.

references.title: Irritable bowel syndrome and chronic fatigue 6 years after giardia infection: a controlled prospective cohort study

conditions.name: Post-Influenza Sequelae (incl. H1N1-Associated Narcolepsy)
conditions.typical_onset: Post-viral fatigue after influenza; narcolepsy onset within months of 2009 H1N1 exposure (infection or AS03-adjuvanted vaccine).
conditions.overview: Influenza can be followed by prolonged post-viral fatigue, and the 2009 H1N1 pandemic was linked to a rise in type 1 narcolepsy — both after natural infection and after the AS03-adjuvanted Pandemrix vaccine — thought to reflect autoimmune loss of hypocretin neurons. Narcolepsy itself is managed with standard symptomatic therapy.
conditions.epidemiology: Population studies in several European countries found increased narcolepsy incidence in children/adolescents after Pandemrix; an increase was also reported after wild-type H1N1 infection.
mechanisms: Autoimmune destruction of hypocretin (orexin) neurons / molecular mimicry
conditions.diagnosis: Narcolepsy: clinical plus sleep studies (MSLT) and low CSF hypocretin; temporal link to H1N1 exposure supports attribution.

treatments.name: Standard narcolepsy therapy (wake-promoting agents, sodium oxybate)
treatments.summary: H1N1-associated narcolepsy is treated as idiopathic narcolepsy — wake-promoting agents (e.g., modafinil) for sleepiness and sodium oxybate for cataplexy — alongside sleep hygiene. The H1N1/Pandemrix association is documented in population studies.

references.title: Risk of narcolepsy in children and young people receiving AS03 adjuvanted pandemic A/H1N1 2009 influenza vaccine: retrospective analysis
AS03 adjuvanted AH1N1 vaccine associated with an abrupt increase in the incidence of childhood narcolepsy in Finland

conditions.name: Post-Herpetic Neuralgia (VZV)
conditions.typical_onset: Neuropathic pain persisting ≥3 months after a herpes zoster (shingles) rash.
conditions.overview: The most common chronic complication of shingles: persistent, often severe neuropathic pain in the affected dermatome. Unlike most post-viral syndromes, PHN has well-established, evidence-based treatments — gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants, and topical lidocaine as first line.
conditions.epidemiology: A frequent complication of herpes zoster, especially in older adults; risk falls with zoster vaccination.
mechanisms: Sensory nerve damage and sensitization from VZV reactivation
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, with pain persisting after a healed zoster rash in the same dermatome.

treatments.name: First-line: gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants, topical lidocaine 5%
treatments.summary: Gabapentin/pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and the lidocaine 5% patch are first-line, supported by RCTs and an AAN practice parameter; capsaicin 8% and opioids are second-line.

references.title: Practice Parameter: Treatment of postherpetic neuralgia — an evidence-based report of the Quality Standards Subcommittee of the American Academy of Neurology
Analgesic therapy in postherpetic neuralgia: a quantitative systematic review

conditions.name: Post-Treatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS)
conditions.typical_onset: Persistent fatigue, pain, and cognitive symptoms ≥6 months after recommended antibiotic treatment for Lyme disease.
conditions.overview: A subset of patients have lingering fatigue, musculoskeletal pain, and cognitive complaints after guideline-recommended antibiotic therapy for Lyme disease. Multiple randomized trials (including the Klempner trials and the European PLEASE trial) show prolonged/repeat antibiotics provide no durable benefit and carry risk, so management is symptom-directed.
conditions.epidemiology: Reported in a minority of treated Lyme disease patients; exact frequency debated.
mechanisms: Residual tissue injury (persistent active infection not supported by trial data)
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical, with documented prior Lyme disease and appropriate prior antibiotic treatment, after excluding other causes.

treatments.name: Symptom-directed care (not prolonged antibiotics)
treatments.summary: Management targets individual symptoms (pain, fatigue, sleep). Randomized trials show extended or repeat antibiotic therapy offers no lasting benefit over placebo and carries risk.

references.title: Randomized Trial of Longer-Term Therapy for Symptoms Attributed to Lyme Disease (PLEASE)
Treatment trials for post-Lyme disease symptoms revisited

conditions.name: Post-Enteroviral Syndrome (Coxsackievirus B)
conditions.typical_onset: Persistent fatigue after enteroviral illness; cardiac symptoms after viral myocarditis.
conditions.overview: Coxsackievirus B is a leading cause of viral myocarditis and has long been linked to post-viral fatigue and ME/CFS. There is no specific antiviral; myocarditis is managed with guideline-directed supportive heart-failure care, and immunomodulatory approaches (IVIG, immunosuppression) remain investigational.
conditions.epidemiology: Coxsackievirus B is among the most common causes of viral myocarditis; a historical trigger implicated in ME/CFS.
mechanisms: Direct viral myocyte injury and viral persistence
conditions.diagnosis: Clinical/serologic; myocarditis confirmed by cardiac imaging/biopsy where indicated.

treatments.name: Supportive, guideline-directed care for myocarditis
treatments.summary: No specific antiviral is established; viral myocarditis is managed with standard heart-failure therapy and supportive care. IVIG and immunosuppression have been studied but remain unproven for routine use.

references.title: Treatment of viral myocarditis caused by coxsackievirus B

comparison.note: Two cross-condition views. The mechanism matrix maps each illness to shared candidate mechanisms; the symptom-frequency matrix compares reported prevalence of core post-viral symptoms. Prevalence figures are not fully standardized across studies (case definitions, denominators, timepoints, and severity thresholds differ), so cells show ranges or qualitative levels and cite their source. Blank / 'insufficient data' cells mean no standardized figure was located — not that the symptom or mechanism is absent. Conditions whose presentations are not on these fatigue-spectrum axes — PANS/PANDAS, post-Zika Guillain-Barré, post-herpetic neuralgia, H1N1-associated narcolepsy, and post-enteroviral myocarditis — are omitted from the symptom-frequency matrix.

mechanism_axes.label: Viral / antigen persistence
mechanism_axes.description: Persistence of intact virus or viral antigen in tissues or immune-privileged sites.

mechanism_axes.label: Immune dysregulation & autoimmunity
mechanism_axes.description: Sustained immune activation, autoantibodies, or molecular mimicry driving autoimmune injury.

mechanism_axes.label: Autonomic dysfunction
mechanism_axes.description: Dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system (e.g., orthostatic intolerance, POTS).

mechanism_axes.label: Neuroinflammation
mechanism_axes.description: Inflammatory activation within the central nervous system.

mechanism_axes.label: Mitochondrial / metabolic
mechanism_axes.description: Impaired cellular energy metabolism and mitochondrial dysfunction.

mechanism_axes.label: Endothelial / microvascular
mechanism_axes.description: Endothelial dysfunction, microclots, or impaired tissue perfusion.

mechanism_axes.label: Latent-virus reactivation
mechanism_axes.description: Reactivation of latent herpesviruses (e.g., EBV, HHV-6).

mechanism_axes.label: Gut microbiome dysbiosis
mechanism_axes.description: Altered gut microbiota composition and intestinal barrier function.

cells.note: Predominant mechanism is distal degeneration of enlarged motor units / ongoing denervation from prior poliovirus injury, not a shared post-viral immune mechanism.
Persistent viral antigen in joint tissue is a leading hypothesis.
Documented Ebola virus persistence in immune-privileged sites (eye, semen).
Autonomic dysfunction is the defining abnormality.
Autoantibody / autoimmune mechanisms proposed in a subset.
Post-infectious autoimmunity / molecular mimicry targeting basal ganglia.
Post-infectious autoimmune attack on peripheral nerves (molecular mimicry).
Neuronal injury with persistent pro-inflammatory cytokine elevation.
Persistent infection not supported: long-term doxycycline was no better than placebo (Qure trial).
Autoimmune loss of hypocretin neurons / molecular mimicry linked to H1N1 exposure.
Mechanism is sensory nerve damage/sensitization from VZV reactivation — neuropathic, not the shared post-viral fatigue mechanisms.
Persistent active infection not supported by randomized retreatment trials.

references.title: Unexplained post-acute infection syndromes
Characterizing long COVID in an international cohort: 7 months of symptoms and their impact
Prevalence and measurement of post-exertional malaise in post-acute COVID-19 syndrome: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Prevalence of somatic symptoms among Ebola Virus Disease (EVD) survivors in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis
Postpolio Syndrome: A Review of Lived Experiences of Patients
references.source: PMC / International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
